channel.title: The Morning Brief
channel.description: Your daily rundown of national headlines, Columbus local news, home lending updates, and the latest in AI. Produced by Jeff Bechtel.
channel.itunes:author: Jeff Bechtel

item.title: Morning Brief — Morning Brief for Friday, April 17, 2026
item.description: U.S.-Iran blockade pressure, CDC leadership turmoil, mortgage rates near four-week lows, Columbus auditor and NWSL deadlines, AI chip demand, market records, and a mild Central Ohio forecast.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Friday, April 17th, and this is your Morning Brief.

The main story this morning is that the U.S.-Iran ceasefire is still alive, but the military pressure around it is expanding. The Associated Press reports the U.S. military has broadened its maritime operation beyond the blockade of Iranian ports, authorizing forces around the world to interdict ships linked to Iran or suspected of carrying materials that could support Tehran's war effort. That includes items well beyond weapons, such as oil, metals, electronics, machinery, and other dual-use cargo.

This is the harder edge of the diplomatic track we have been following all week. Pakistan's army chief was in Tehran pushing for another round of U.S.-Iran talks before the ceasefire expires on April 22nd. Regional officials have said there is an in-principle agreement to extend the pause for more diplomacy, but Iran has also threatened to disrupt regional trade if the blockade is not lifted. So the situation is moving in two directions at once: mediators are trying to keep talks on the calendar, while the United States is widening enforcement at sea.

That matters because the ceasefire is no longer just a battlefield question. It is an energy-market question, a shipping-insurance question, and a household inflation question. If Iran and the United States agree on a next negotiating round, oil can lose some of its war premium. If inspections, seizures, or a Gulf confrontation escalate, the price of crude and gasoline can move quickly. The watch item for the weekend is whether diplomacy produces a formal meeting site, likely Islamabad, or whether the blockade becomes the dominant signal.

The other national story to keep on the board is the CDC. The Guardian reports the agency is dealing with a severe leadership vacuum, with many top roles still unfilled and morale damaged after departures, firings, and vaccine-policy fights under Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. The Atlantic reports the administration has nominated Rear Admiral Erica Schwartz to lead the CDC, a move meant to stabilize an agency that has been operating without a confirmed director.

The immediate issue is public-health capacity. The CDC is the agency that tracks outbreaks, supports state health departments, sets vaccine guidance, and helps coordinate responses when disease, weather disasters, or contaminated products cross state lines. Political fights over vaccines get the headlines, but the operational concern is broader: whether basic decisions, data releases, and program approvals are slowing down because there are not enough permanent leaders with authority to move work through the system.

For households and local governments, this is a reminder that national institutions matter most when nobody is thinking about them. A quiet CDC is not automatically a stable CDC. If measles, avian flu, foodborne illness, or a new respiratory threat accelerates, the country needs fast guidance that local officials can trust. The Schwartz nomination is therefore more than a personnel item. It is a test of whether the administration can rebuild confidence inside an agency that is supposed to be boring, competent, and ready.

Now to home lending. Mortgage rates improved again, but the improvement is still small relative to the affordability problem. Freddie Mac's April 16th survey put the 30-year fixed mortgage at 6.30 percent, down from 6.37 percent a week earlier and below 6.83 percent a year ago. Bankrate's same-day survey had the average 30-year fixed at 6.38 percent, with the 15-year fixed at 5.77 percent. NerdWallet's Zillow-based data from Wednesday afternoon showed a lower 30-year fixed average near 6.05 percent APR.

The practical read is that buyers are in a low-to-mid sixes market, depending on data source, credit profile, loan type, points, and lender appetite. That is better than the worst levels of the last few years, but it is not cheap money. In Central Ohio, the rate only tells part of the story. Property taxes, insurance, maintenance, HOA dues, and the actual purchase price still determine whether a payment is durable.

If you are under contract, ask for the locked rate, APR, points, lender credit, and float-down rules in writing. If you are shopping, run the payment at today's quote and then again a quarter point higher. A deal that only works at the best rate on the screen is not a resilient deal. For sellers, the message is also straightforward: the buyer is negotiating the monthly payment, not just the headline price. A price cut, a closing-cost credit, and a temporary buydown can all help, but they are not interchangeable. The cleanest strategy is the one that makes the payment easy to understand.

In Columbus, the City Auditor transition remains the local government story with the biggest operating consequences. Megan Kilgore is resigning effective May 4th for a private-sector role connected to the municipal bond market. WOSU reports Columbus City Council will appoint her replacement, and Council President Shannon Hardin has already outlined a next-step process after praising Kilgore's tenure.

The reason this matters is that the auditor's office is not ceremonial. It manages the city's financial record, revenue systems, accounting operations, income-tax collection, payroll systems, debt issuance, investment oversight, and investor relations. WOSU reported the office oversees roughly 1.2 billion dollars in annual income-tax collections, a 5 billion dollar debt portfolio, and a 2.5 billion dollar investment portfolio. In plain English, this office is part of the machinery that lets Columbus borrow, budget, pay employees, and defend its credit rating.

The continuity language from earlier this week still applies: the next auditor needs credibility in public finance, not just political proximity. Council can move quickly and still show its work. Watch for whether members announce an application window, public hearing, interim appointment, or selection criteria. The city has less than three weeks before Kilgore's exit date, so silence would become its own signal.

The next hard local deadline is Monday, April 20th, when Columbus City Council may take up the NWSL-related stadium and training-facility package. The Columbus Dispatch reports the Southwest Area Commission passed a resolution opposing any ordinance or action that would give away, trade, reduce, or repurpose McCoy Park for the proposed training facility. Area commissions are advisory, so that resolution does not stop Council by itself. But it puts neighborhood opposition directly in front of a vote.

The proposal connects several things: Columbus' bid for a National Women's Soccer League expansion team, the Haslam Sports Group's role, a possible training facility on McCoy Park land, and a two percent event tax at ScottsMiracle-Gro Field that would help repay 25 million dollars in project costs. Supporters see a chance to land top-tier women's soccer and strengthen the city's sports profile. Opponents see a public park in Southwest Columbus being narrowed for a private sports bid before residents have given clear and direct consent.

That is why this story should not be flattened into pro-soccer versus anti-soccer. It is about process, land, public money, and neighborhood trust. Columbus already has a women's soccer ecosystem, including the Columbus Eagles, and any NWSL effort should explain how it strengthens what already exists. If Council votes Monday, the key questions are whether the park land changes, whether the tax structure changes, and whether residents get enforceable protections rather than verbal assurances.

The May 5th crisis-response charter amendment is also moving toward voters. Axios Columbus reports the measure would establish a round-the-clock alternative response program for some 911 and non-emergency calls, especially those involving mental or behavioral health needs. The measure came out of a compromise between Columbus officials and the Columbus Safety Collective, and Axios reported there was no organized opposition as of this week.

The basic idea is simple: not every call needs a police-only or fire-only response. Some calls need clinicians, social workers, peer supporters, EMTs, or other trained responders who can de-escalate and connect people to care. The implementation is the hard part. Columbus would have to build staffing, dispatch rules, safety protocols, overnight coverage, training, data reporting, and coordination with county and state systems. Supporters point to the need for care-first responses. Skeptics will want to know how calls are screened, how responders are protected, and how success will be measured.

This is a ballot issue with a long runway. If voters approve it, the city would still need years to scale the system. But the charter question matters because it changes the obligation from a program that can be expanded or neglected in annual budgets to a citywide commitment that future officials have to implement.

On technology, the AI boom is becoming less abstract by the day. TSMC, the world's leading contract chipmaker, reported a 58.3 percent jump in quarterly profit, according to the Associated Press, with net profit reaching about 18.1 billion dollars. The driver was demand for advanced semiconductors used in artificial intelligence. TSMC also projected stronger second-quarter revenue and pointed to heavy capital spending for new capacity in Taiwan, Japan, and the United States.

The important detail is that AI growth is now an industrial story. It is about 3-nanometer production, advanced packaging, helium supply, fabrication plants, power contracts, water use, and logistics. TSMC also warned that the Iran war could raise costs or pressure chipmaking materials even if near-term operations are buffered by inventory. That connects the AI supply chain directly to the energy and shipping risk we started with.

Meta and Broadcom are another signal. Meta announced an expanded partnership with Broadcom to co-develop multiple generations of custom AI silicon for Meta's own infrastructure. The agreement includes a commitment of more than one gigawatt in an initial phase, with a larger multi-gigawatt rollout planned. That tells you where the largest AI companies are going: custom chips, custom networking, and custom data centers built around their own workloads.

For Ohio, the local version is the data-center debate. AI infrastructure needs land, substations, transmission upgrades, water, tax incentives, and reliable power. Communities want jobs and investment, but residents also want to know who pays for grid upgrades and whether local rates, farmland, noise, or water systems become the hidden cost of someone else's cloud. Expect that debate to keep growing in Central Ohio as AI demand keeps turning into physical construction.

Now the market snapshot. U.S. stocks kept climbing Thursday. AP says the S&P 500 rose 0.3 percent to 7,041.28, another record close. The Dow gained 0.2 percent to 48,578.72. The Nasdaq rose 0.4 percent to 24,102.70. The rally has been powered by two forces: hope that the Iran war does not create the worst-case energy shock, and strong corporate earnings tied especially to banks and AI infrastructure.

But the risk has not disappeared. Brent crude has moved back near 99 dollars a barrel, gold remains elevated, and Treasury yields have edged higher. That combination says investors are willing to own stocks, but they are not treating the ceasefire as settled. For retirement accounts, the key read is balance. A record high is good news, but a market this tied to diplomacy and oil can reverse quickly if the weekend produces a bad headline from the Gulf.

For Central Ohio weather, today is the calmer day between storm chances. The National Weather Service forecast for Columbus calls for mostly sunny skies and a high near 80, with light west wind. Weather Street and Time and Date are in the same neighborhood, with highs in the upper 70s to around 80 and mostly cloudy to partly cloudy conditions. After Thursday night's showers, Friday should give us a usable spring day.

The weekend is more unsettled. Rain and thunderstorms return late tonight into Saturday, with the National Weather Service showing showers and possibly a thunderstorm Saturday and a high near 78. Sunday turns cooler, with sunshine returning and highs closer to the mid-50s. If you have yard work, a school event, or travel across I-70, I-71, 315, or the outerbelt, today is the better window. Saturday is the day to keep the radar close.

The through-line this morning is deadlines. The U.S.-Iran ceasefire expires April 22nd unless mediators lock in more talks. Columbus City Council may vote on the NWSL package April 20th. Housing data arrives April 21st with pending sales. Megan Kilgore leaves the auditor's office May 4th. Columbus voters decide the crisis-response charter amendment May 5th. And underneath all of it, AI investment, oil risk, mortgage rates, and public trust are pushing on the same household budgets.

That is your Morning Brief for Friday, April 17th. Watch for a formal U.S.-Iran talks announcement, any Council signal on the auditor appointment, whether the McCoy Park package changes before Monday, and Saturday's storms before you commit to outdoor plans.

item.title: Morning Brief — Morning Brief for Thursday, April 16, 2026
item.description: Iran ceasefire diplomacy and oil risk, mortgage rates and buyer math, Columbus auditor succession, crisis response and NWSL funding, AI chip earnings, markets, and a warm stormy Central Ohio forecast.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Thursday, April 16th, and this is your Morning Brief.

The biggest story this morning is still the effort to keep the U.S.-Iran ceasefire alive while the American naval blockade of Iranian ports remains in place. The Associated Press reports Pakistan's army chief is in Tehran today, trying to reinforce the diplomatic track after mediators said the United States and Iran had given an in-principle agreement to extend the truce for more talks. The current ceasefire expires on April 22nd, so this is no longer a distant deadline. It is the central clock on the world economy this week.

The tension is that diplomacy and escalation are moving at the same time. The U.S. blockade is now being enforced against ships tied to Iranian ports. American officials say the goal is to isolate Iranian maritime trade while letting non-Iranian traffic keep moving. Iran says the blockade could violate the ceasefire and has threatened to disrupt Gulf trade if it continues. China is calling for the Strait of Hormuz to reopen. Shipping companies, insurers, energy buyers, and regional governments are trying to decide whether the next move is a diplomatic extension or a wider maritime confrontation.

That distinction matters because oil has become the real-time gauge of whether markets believe the war is cooling or widening. Brent crude is still around the mid-90s, and U.S. crude is around the low 90s. That is below the panic levels seen earlier in the conflict, but far above the pre-war price near 70 dollars. If talks firm up, the risk premium can fade. If Iran moves against regional trade or if the blockade starts producing confrontations at sea, fuel prices can move quickly the other way.

The inflation read is still mixed. Producer prices for March were softer in some underlying categories, but energy is doing enough damage to keep the Federal Reserve cautious. The Fed does not set oil prices, and it cannot negotiate a ceasefire. But it does have to decide whether higher energy costs stay temporary or start feeding into consumer expectations, business pricing, and wage demands. That is why the April 28th and 29th Fed meeting still matters, even though the real pressure point this week is overseas.

For households, the most obvious channel is gasoline. The next one is borrowing. Mortgage rates improved a little this week, but not enough to change the affordability problem. Bankrate's April 15th survey put the average 30-year fixed mortgage at 6.38 percent, down from 6.45 percent a week earlier, with the 15-year fixed at 5.78 percent. Fortune's Optimal Blue data had the 30-year conforming rate at 6.279 percent for loans locked as of April 13th. NerdWallet, using a different data set, showed a lower 30-year average near 6.07 percent Wednesday evening.

The practical translation is that borrowers are still in a fragmented low-to-mid sixes market. A buyer with strong credit, enough down payment, and a clean file may see offers closer to the low sixes. A broader national average still sits higher. For Central Ohio buyers, that means the rate quote matters, but the full monthly payment matters more. Taxes, insurance, HOA dues, repair reserves, and commuting costs can wipe out the benefit of a slightly better rate.

So the home-lending advice remains disciplined. If you are under contract, compare lock options and ask the lender how float-down language actually works. If you are still shopping, run your payment at today's quote and then stress-test it a quarter point higher. If the deal only works at the best rate on the screen, it probably does not work yet. Sellers should pay attention too. A price cut, a closing-cost credit, or a rate buydown can all change the monthly payment, but they do not feel the same to a buyer. The cleanest offer may be the one that makes the payment understandable.

In Columbus, the City Auditor transition is still the most important government story because it touches the financial machinery behind almost everything else. Megan Kilgore submitted her resignation this week, effective May 4th, after winning reelection only five months ago. WOSU reports she is leaving for a private-sector role tied to the municipal bond market, and Columbus City Council will appoint her replacement.

This is not just a personnel move. The auditor's office handles revenue collection, city accounting, payroll, investment oversight, debt issuance, and investor relations. WOSU notes the office oversees about 1.2 billion dollars in annual income-tax collections, a 5 billion dollar debt portfolio, and a 2.5 billion dollar investment portfolio. The city also points to its triple-A credit ratings as part of Kilgore's legacy.

The continuity question is what Council does next. Does it appoint a caretaker to keep the office steady? Does it elevate someone with deep public-finance experience? Or does it appoint someone who immediately becomes the favorite for the next full election cycle? Mayor Andrew Ginther has called for an open and transparent public engagement process. That phrase is worth watching, because Columbus now has to move quickly without making the appointment look pre-decided.

The other Columbus story with a hard near-term date is the National Women's Soccer League bid and the proposed McCoy Park training facility. Yesterday, the Southwest Area Commission passed a resolution opposing any deal that would give away, trade, reduce, or repurpose McCoy Park land for the project. Area commissions are advisory, so the resolution does not block City Council by itself. But it changes the politics before a possible April 20th vote.

The proposed structure would support Columbus' NWSL expansion bid backed by the Haslam Sports Group, with a 2 percent event tax at ScottsMiracle-Gro Field helping repay 25 million dollars for a training facility on McCoy Park land. Supporters see the project as a way to strengthen the bid and bring top-tier women's soccer to Columbus. Opponents in Southwest Columbus see a limited green-space asset being narrowed without enough direct neighborhood consent.

This is now three stories at once. It is a sports story, because Columbus wants to compete for an NWSL team. It is a public-finance story, because event-tax revenue and city-backed facilities deserve scrutiny. And it is a neighborhood story, because McCoy Park residents are asking why growth so often lands as a tradeoff on their side of town. The Columbus Eagles also remain part of the local soccer context. They have built women's soccer here for years, and the city will need to show that a national expansion bid strengthens the existing ecosystem instead of stepping over it.

The May 5th emergency response charter amendment is also moving toward voters. The measure, listed by Franklin County as Proposed Charter Amendment Number 2, would create a community crisis response system for emergency and non-emergency calls that need something other than a traditional police-only or fire-only dispatch. The Columbus Safety Collective says the goal is to send trained non-police responders, including clinicians, social workers, peer supporters, and EMTs, to appropriate behavioral health, wellness-check, homelessness, and related calls.

The continuity point from earlier this week is that this is the compromise measure between city officials and the Safety Collective. Axios reports there is still no organized opposition campaign, but that does not mean the implementation questions are small. Voters are being asked to put a requirement into the charter. The operational work still comes after that: staffing, call screening, dispatch rules, overnight coverage, coordination with county and state services, and how to report results in a way residents can understand.

On technology, the AI boom is showing up in very concrete earnings and infrastructure numbers. TSMC reported a 58 percent jump in quarterly profit, according to AP, driven by demand for advanced chips used in artificial intelligence. The company also raised its second-quarter revenue outlook and is still pouring money into new capacity in Taiwan, Japan, and the United States. TSMC is the quiet center of the global AI buildout because it manufactures the advanced chips behind many of the products people talk about more often.

The important detail is that AI demand is not slowing into a normal tech cycle. It is becoming an industrial cycle. Advanced packaging, 3-nanometer chip production, helium supply, factory capacity, power availability, and data-center construction are all part of the same chain. TSMC warned that the Iran war could raise costs or pressure supply chains for key materials, even as demand remains strong. That connects the AI story right back to the national-security story.

Meta and Broadcom are another example. Meta announced an expanded partnership with Broadcom to co-develop multiple generations of custom AI chips for Meta's own infrastructure. The agreement includes an initial deployment of more than 1 gigawatt of compute, with a larger multi-gigawatt rollout planned. Broadcom CEO Hock Tan is leaving Meta's board and moving into an advisory role tied to the custom silicon roadmap.

That is a large signal about where AI infrastructure is going. The biggest AI companies do not want to depend only on off-the-shelf accelerators. They want custom chips, custom networking, custom data-center designs, and more control over cost per query. For Ohio, that keeps the data-center debate alive. Communities want investment, jobs, and tax base. They also want answers on water, power, land use, grid costs, noise, and whether residents end up subsidizing infrastructure for some of the richest companies in the world.

Now the market snapshot. Wall Street hit new records Wednesday as investors bet that diplomacy can prevent the worst economic outcomes from the Iran war. AP says the S&P 500 rose 0.8 percent to 7,022.95, a new record close. The Nasdaq gained 1.6 percent to 24,016.02, while the Dow slipped slightly to 48,463.72. The rally has been sharp because the S&P had fallen nearly 10 percent below its record in late March, then climbed back as ceasefire hopes improved.

This morning, futures are basically flat. Yahoo Finance showed S&P futures near 7,006, Dow futures around 48,743, and Nasdaq futures just above 26,000. Gold is still elevated near 4,846 dollars an ounce, Bitcoin is around 74,300 dollars, and crude remains near 91 dollars. The market message is not that risk is gone. It is that investors think the base case is diplomacy, while still paying up for safety in gold and keeping oil well above normal.

For Central Ohio weather, today looks warm, breezy, and unsettled. AccuWeather has Columbus near 75 for the high, with considerable cloudiness, a couple of showers, and a thunderstorm possible. Any heavier downpour could bring gusty winds or small hail. Weather Street's hourly forecast is similar, with southwest to west-southwest winds, mostly cloudy skies, and on-and-off rain chances through the day.

This is not the same near-record heat as yesterday, when Columbus was around 84. But it is still warmer than normal for mid-April, and the atmosphere has enough spring energy to make the radar worth checking before school pickup, errands, or the evening commute. Keep the rain jacket close, and do not assume dry pavement at lunch means dry pavement for the ride home.

Friday looks more manageable, with a break before another weekend system. Saturday brings the next organized rain chance, and then cooler air may push in behind it. The practical read: today is a mild but messy spring day. Plan around showers, watch for gusty storms, and give yourself extra room on I-70, I-71, 315, and the outerbelt if downpours line up during drive time.

The through-line this morning is pressure with deadlines. The Iran ceasefire is being tested before April 22nd. Mortgage borrowers are watching small rate moves that matter because prices are already stretched. Columbus has to choose an auditor successor and decide whether the NWSL bid can survive neighborhood objections. Voters are less than three weeks from deciding the crisis-response amendment. AI demand is pushing chips, power, water, and local land-use debates into one system.

The dates to keep in mind are April 20th for the possible Columbus NWSL funding vote, April 21st for the next housing-market read on pending sales, April 22nd for the Iran ceasefire deadline, May 4th for Kilgore's resignation, and May 5th for the Columbus crisis-response ballot issue.

That is your Morning Brief for Thursday, April 16th. Keep an eye on the Iran talks, the oil price, the radar, and whether Columbus City Council signals how it plans to handle the auditor appointment and the McCoy Park vote.

item.title: Morning Brief — Morning Brief for Wednesday, April 15, 2026
item.description: U.S.-Iran blockade talks, inflation and mortgage rates, Columbus auditor transition, emergency response and NWSL funding, AI chips and bank cybersecurity, markets, and a warm stormy Central Ohio forecast.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Wednesday, April 15th, and this is your Morning Brief.

The biggest story this morning is still the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports, but the tone has shifted slightly from shock to test. The Associated Press reports that the U.S. military says the blockade is now fully implemented. Washington says the goal is to stop maritime trade tied to Iranian ports while keeping traffic to non-Iranian ports moving. That is the line the administration wants the world to see: pressure on Tehran, not a full shutdown of Gulf commerce.

The problem is that the region does not always respect narrow lines. The Washington Post reports that several merchant ships were turned back in the first day of enforcement, and the U.S. is sending thousands more troops into the Middle East as the fragile ceasefire approaches an April 22nd expiration. Iran has threatened wider disruption if the blockade continues. China has criticized the move. Shipping firms, insurers, energy buyers, and Gulf governments are now trying to judge whether this remains a controlled pressure campaign or becomes the next phase of the war.

There is still a diplomatic channel. AP reports mediators are moving closer to a possible ceasefire extension, and President Trump has said the war is very close to over. Pakistan is still central to the talks, and U.S. officials have floated the possibility of another round within days. That makes the next forty-eight hours unusually important. If the parties can extend the ceasefire while the blockade holds, oil markets may treat this as a painful but temporary squeeze. If talks fail, the blockade becomes harder to separate from escalation.

For Americans, the connection is not abstract. The first place this shows up is energy. Oil is off the worst panic levels from earlier in the week, but it is still expensive. MarketWatch had West Texas Intermediate around 91 dollars a barrel and Brent around 95 dollars this morning, with traders trying to price two opposite outcomes at once: a deal that lowers the risk premium, or a breakdown that sends fuel costs higher again. This is why gasoline, airline tickets, freight costs, and inflation expectations are all part of the same story.

That leads into yesterday’s inflation data. The Bureau of Labor Statistics said the Producer Price Index rose 0.5 percent in March, and wholesale prices were up 4.0 percent from a year earlier. The detail that matters most is energy. Goods prices rose 1.6 percent, energy prices rose 8.5 percent, and services were flat. That means the economy is not flashing the same heat everywhere, but energy is large enough to keep pressure on the whole inflation picture.

For the Federal Reserve, this is an awkward mix. Growth worries argue for flexibility. Energy inflation argues for caution. Markets are still watching the April 28th and 29th Fed meeting as a moment when policymakers will have to acknowledge both sides. The Fed does not control oil tankers or diplomacy, but it does have to decide how much of this price pressure is temporary and how much can bleed into expectations, wages, and consumer behavior.

That same tension is showing up in home lending. Mortgage rates are a little better than last week, but not dramatically better. Bankrate’s April 15th survey put the average 30-year fixed mortgage at 6.38 percent, down from 6.45 percent a week earlier. The 15-year fixed was 5.78 percent. Fortune’s Optimal Blue data, looking at loans locked as of April 13th, had the 30-year conforming rate closer to 6.279 percent and the 15-year at 5.689 percent. Freddie Mac’s most recent weekly survey, from April 9th, had the 30-year at 6.37 percent.

The practical read is that borrowers are still in a low-to-mid sixes market, with different sources showing different daily snapshots. That is better than the highs of last year, but it is not the kind of relief that fixes affordability by itself. For a Central Ohio buyer, a small move from 6.45 to 6.38 percent helps, but it does not erase taxes, insurance, repair costs, or a list price that already assumes Columbus-area demand will stay strong.

So the lending advice is less about guessing the perfect rate and more about controlling the parts you can control. Buyers who are already under contract should stay close to their lender and compare lock options, especially if their closing date is near. Buyers still shopping should run payment scenarios with taxes and insurance included, not just principal and interest. Sellers should remember that rate-sensitive buyers notice every 10 or 20 basis points. If a house is not moving, the issue may be the monthly payment, not the number of showings.

In Columbus, the freshest local government story is a surprise transition at City Hall. WOSU reports City Auditor Megan Kilgore submitted her resignation Tuesday morning, just five months after voters re-elected her. She is leaving for a private-sector role tied to the municipal bond market and said she will split time between Columbus and New York. Her resignation is effective May 4th, and Columbus City Council will appoint a replacement.

This matters because the auditor’s office is not a ceremonial job. It handles revenue collection, city accounting, payroll, investment oversight, debt issuance, and investor relations. Kilgore’s office has also been part of the story Columbus tells credit-rating agencies, including its triple-A ratings and financial reporting speed. The next question is whether Council appoints a caretaker, a long-term successor, or someone who immediately becomes the political favorite for the next election cycle. Watch for the appointment timeline and for any signal from Council President Shannon Hardin or Mayor Andrew Ginther about continuity in the finance office.

The other Columbus story with a near-term date is the May 5th emergency response charter amendment. Voters will decide whether the city should create a 24/7 non-police response option for some crisis calls. The concept is to route some calls toward social workers and mental or behavioral health professionals instead of defaulting to police. The city already has alternative-response programs, but they are not available around the clock. Supporters say the amendment would make that coverage more consistent and reduce unnecessary police contact.

The continuity point from yesterday still holds: this is a compromise version of a larger proposal. City officials and the Columbus Safety Collective are aligned behind the ballot language, and there has not been a major organized opposition campaign reported yet. The watch points are funding, staffing, dispatch rules, and turnout. A charter amendment can create a mandate, but the operational question is how Columbus decides which calls qualify, who responds, and how quickly the city can staff a full-time model.

Columbus City Council also has another developing story on the sports and development front. The city’s National Women’s Soccer League bid remains tied to a proposed funding structure around ScottsMiracle-Gro Field and McCoy Park. The emerging plan includes a 2 percent event tax at Crew games and other events, with revenue helping repay 25 million dollars for a training facility on vacant McCoy Park land. A City Council vote could come as soon as April 20th.

This is a soccer story, but it is also a neighborhood and public-finance story. The Haslam Sports Group-backed bid could put Columbus in a stronger position for NWSL expansion, but residents around McCoy Park will want to know what the facility does to traffic, public access, green space, and future development pressure. The Columbus Eagles also remain part of the local context, because they have carried the women’s soccer pipeline here for years. The question is not only whether Columbus lands a top-tier team. It is whether the city can connect the new investment to the existing soccer community without treating it as disposable.

On technology, the clearest market signal is coming from the chip supply chain. Reuters reports TSMC is expected to post a fourth straight quarter of record profit, with analysts looking for about a 50 percent jump in net profit for the January-to-March quarter. The driver is exactly what you would expect: demand for artificial intelligence infrastructure, including advanced 3-nanometer chips and packaging capacity that still cannot keep up with customer needs.

That is the clean version of the AI boom. The messy version is what happens when chip demand turns into land demand, power demand, water demand, and local political pressure. Central Ohio is already living that question through data centers. Communities want investment and tax base, but they also want answers on grid costs, water use, zoning, noise, and whether residents end up subsidizing infrastructure for some of the richest companies in the world. The watch point in Ohio remains whether organizers pushing a constitutional amendment on data centers show signature momentum before the July 1st deadline, and whether utilities or regulators get more explicit about who pays for the next wave of grid upgrades.

There is also a specific AI deal in the market this morning. Investopedia reports Broadcom shares rose after the company announced an expanded partnership with Meta to co-develop AI chips. Broadcom CEO Hock Tan is stepping down from Meta’s board to become a special advisor as that work grows. The significance is that the biggest AI customers are not just buying whatever chip supply exists. They are trying to shape custom silicon, reduce dependence on a few suppliers, and control more of the hardware stack behind their models.

The second AI story is cybersecurity. The tracker has been following Anthropic’s restricted Mythos model because the question is not only what frontier AI can do in a demo. It is what happens when financial institutions use it to find vulnerabilities in real systems. The live issue is whether banks and regulators can safely test advanced models against old code, payment rails, vendor software, and known vulnerability data without creating new offensive risk. If a model can help defenders find weak points faster, a similar capability could help attackers do the same. That is why access controls, logging, audit rules, and regulator guidance matter as much as the headline capability.

Now the market snapshot. U.S. futures were quiet this morning, with the major indexes near flat as investors watched Iran talks and bank earnings. AP reported the S&P 500 and Dow futures were little changed while oil ticked higher. Yahoo Finance showed S&P futures near 7,004, Dow futures around 48,733, and Nasdaq futures just under 26,000. Gold was around 4,850 dollars an ounce, showing that safety demand is still present even as stocks remain resilient. Bitcoin was around 74,265 dollars. Bank of America and Morgan Stanley earnings were better than expected, adding to the sense that big banks are holding up even while executives remain cautious about energy prices and geopolitical risk.

Today is also Tax Day for most individual filers. If you have not filed or requested an extension, the practical deadline is today. An extension gives more time to file paperwork, but it does not give more time to pay what is owed without potential penalties and interest. That is the personal-finance footnote on a day when markets are watching oil, banks, and Washington all at once.

For Central Ohio weather, plan for a very warm, breezy, unsettled day. AccuWeather’s Columbus forecast has a high near 84, which would tie the record for the date from 2010, with intervals of clouds and sun and a thunderstorm possible. Winds should be out of the southwest, and the broader Ohio Valley setup supports scattered storms later today and tonight. The biggest concerns are gusty wind, heavy downpours, lightning, and small hail in stronger cells. It is not a guaranteed all-day washout, but it is a check-the-radar-before-the-commute kind of forecast.

Thursday stays warm, cloudy, and stormy, with a higher chance of showers and thunderstorms and the possibility of gusty winds or small hail in any downpour. Friday looks more manageable before another system approaches for the weekend. The bottom line: enjoy the warmth, but do not treat it like a settled summer day. Keep an umbrella or rain jacket nearby, and give yourself extra time if storms line up near I-70, I-71, 315, or the outerbelt.

The through-line this morning is controlled pressure. The U.S. is trying to pressure Iran without breaking global shipping. Inflation is pressuring the Fed without looking uniform across the whole economy. Mortgage rates are giving buyers slight relief without solving affordability. Columbus is managing leadership change, emergency-response reform, and sports development at the same time. AI is pushing profit, power demand, chip strategy, and cyber risk into one conversation.

The dates to keep in mind are April 20th for the possible Columbus NWSL funding vote, April 22nd for the Iran ceasefire deadline, April 28th and 29th for the Fed meeting, May 4th for Kilgore’s resignation, and May 5th for the Columbus emergency-response ballot issue.

That is your Morning Brief for Wednesday, April 15th. Keep an eye on oil, radar, mortgage locks, and any signal that the Iran talks are moving from optimism to an actual extension.

item.title: Morning Brief — Morning Brief for Tuesday, April 14, 2026
item.description: U.S.-Iran blockade pressure, inflation and mortgage rates, Columbus emergency response ballot, AI cyber risk, markets, and a warm stormy Central Ohio forecast.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It is Tuesday, April 14th, and this is your Morning Brief.

The big national story is still the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports, and the most important update this morning is that the action has now moved from warning to reality. The Associated Press reports that President Trump said the American military has begun blocking Iranian ports as part of the pressure campaign to force Tehran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and accept a deal to end the war. U.S. Central Command has said ships moving to and from non-Iranian ports should not be impeded, but vessels tied to Iranian ports and coastal areas are the focus. That distinction matters because it is the line Washington is trying to draw between a blockade of Iran and a full closure of the global oil chokepoint.

Iran is not treating that line as reassuring. Tehran has threatened ports across the Persian Gulf and Gulf of Oman, putting pressure on U.S.-aligned countries that depend on those shipping routes. Vessel tracking cited by the AP showed at least two tankers approaching the strait turning around soon after the blockade began. The U.K. Maritime Trade Operations agency said the blockade covered the Iranian coastline, including ports and energy infrastructure, while transit to and from non-Iranian places was not reported to be blocked.

The diplomatic picture is still active, which is why markets are not reacting in one straight line. AP says Pakistan is still working to mediate another round of U.S.-Iran talks. NPR’s reporting says France and Britain are preparing a separate, defensive navigation effort that could be used if conditions allow. The immediate question is whether any channel can keep the ceasefire from cracking while U.S. ships enforce the blockade and Iran decides how far to push back.

The domestic political risk for Washington is that this is no longer an overseas story people can ignore. If the blockade stays narrow, the White House can argue it is using pressure to reopen navigation and force negotiations. If it widens, or if Iran hits Gulf infrastructure, the administration owns the next round of economic pain at home. That is why the next twenty-four to forty-eight hours matter. Watch for three signals: whether insurers and shipping companies pull back further, whether Gulf allies publicly ask Washington to adjust the operation, and whether congressional criticism starts moving from party-line reaction to concern about fuel prices and escalation authority.

For consumers, the fastest transmission belt is still energy. Brent crude jumped above 100 dollars a barrel on Monday after the blockade announcement, then eased back under that level in early Tuesday trade on hopes that diplomacy is not dead. The move is still large enough to keep gasoline, shipping, airline fuel, and inflation expectations on watch. The key phrase for the next few days is not just oil price; it is oil volatility.

That brings us to the inflation data. The Producer Price Index for March came out this morning, and MarketWatch reports wholesale prices rose 0.5 percent for the month, pushing the annual rate to 4.0 percent, the highest in three years. Core wholesale prices, excluding food and energy, rose a more moderate 0.2 percent. So the story is not that every corner of the economy is suddenly overheating. It is that energy is powerful enough to keep the Federal Reserve boxed in. If fuel-driven costs keep moving through wholesale prices, the Fed has less room to cut rates even if consumers and businesses start slowing down.

That matters for home lending. Mortgage rates are not moving in perfect lockstep across surveys, but the message is consistent: relief is limited and fragile. Fortune, using Optimal Blue lock data available April 13th, put the average 30-year conforming fixed mortgage at 6.267 percent and the 15-year at 5.802 percent. NerdWallet’s Zillow-based snapshot had the 30-year fixed around 6.18 percent APR and the 15-year around 5.66 percent APR this morning. Bankrate’s rate cited Monday was higher, with the 30-year fixed at 6.41 percent and the 15-year at 5.78 percent.

For buyers, the practical point is that rates are still close enough to the low-sixes to keep some spring deals alive, but not low enough to erase affordability pressure. If oil stabilizes and recession fear grows, mortgage rates could ease. If the blockade pushes energy inflation into another round of hot data, lenders may not have much room to improve pricing. For Central Ohio, where buyers are already stretching budgets and looking farther out from Columbus, that keeps the same split market in place: rate-sensitive buyers wait, cash-heavy and high-income buyers stay active, and sellers have to price carefully if they want movement before summer.

In Columbus, the freshest local story is the May 5th ballot question that could change how the city responds to some 911 calls. Axios Columbus reports voters will decide on a city charter amendment to create a 24/7 program that sends some crisis calls toward non-police responders, including social workers and mental and behavioral health professionals. The city already has alternative response programs, but a consultant review found they are not available around the clock. Supporters say the measure would reduce unnecessary police interactions and connect people in crisis with better-fit help.

This is also a political compromise story. City officials and the Columbus Safety Collective jointly kicked off the campaign after agreeing on scaled-back ballot language. Axios reports there does not appear to be organized opposition right now, but the measure is smaller than the initial proposal that sought an eventual 12 million dollar commitment. If voters approve it, the goal would be to expand the program by 2028 and make it fully operational by 2030. The near-term watch is whether the low-conflict campaign stays that way as early voting continues.

The practical question for Columbus residents is not just whether the idea sounds good; it is what the handoff looks like in real time. The measure is aimed at calls where a police response may not be the best first answer, but any twenty-four-hour system has to sort quickly between mental health crisis, public safety risk, medical need, and situations that start as one thing and become another. That means staffing, dispatch rules, union buy-in, and accountability will matter as much as the ballot language. If the campaign stays quiet, the next real debate may come after passage, when city leaders have to turn a charter mandate into an operating model.

The other Columbus story to keep in the frame is the National Women’s Soccer League bid. Yesterday, Axios looked at what a Haslam Sports Group-backed NWSL push could mean for the Columbus Eagles, the lower-division women’s team that has kept the local women’s soccer pipeline alive since 2014. The city’s emerging plan includes a proposed 2 percent tax on Crew games and other events at ScottsMiracle-Gro Field, with revenue used to pay back 25 million dollars for a training facility on vacant McCoy Park land. The existing tracker note still applies: the public funding question, the McCoy Park neighborhood reaction, and the Eagles’ place in the future ecosystem are all moving parts. A City Council vote could come April 20th, so this story is likely to develop within the next week.

On technology and AI, there are two stories worth separating. The first is infrastructure. Reuters reported that TSMC is expected to post a fourth straight quarter of record profit when it reports this week, powered by AI chip demand and advanced packaging capacity that still cannot keep up with what customers want. That is the cleanest reminder that the AI buildout is still a physical supply-chain story: chips, packaging, power, cooling, land, and grid capacity.

That connects directly back to Ohio, where data center growth has turned into a power and local-control fight. Central Ohio communities are seeing the upside of investment and tax base, but also the downside of huge electricity demand, land-use strain, and suspicion that existing residents could end up carrying grid costs. The watch point remains whether organizers behind the proposed Ohio constitutional amendment show signature momentum before the July 1st deadline, and whether utilities or regulators become more explicit about who pays for the upgrades that AI infrastructure needs.

The second AI story is cyber risk. TechCrunch reported that federal officials may be encouraging major banks to test Anthropic’s restricted Mythos model to find vulnerabilities. Reuters-linked reporting from this week said experts are worried that Mythos-like systems could also help attackers discover and chain vulnerabilities in old and new banking systems. Anthropic is limiting access through a defensive program, but the policy question is broader: if a model is strong enough to help banks harden their systems, it may also be strong enough to raise the stakes if similar capability gets loose.

This is where the AI conversation is shifting from novelty to governance. Banks already run red-team exercises and threat modeling, but a frontier model that can reason across old code, vendor systems, and public vulnerability data changes the scale of what a defensive test might find. The upside is faster discovery before criminals get there. The downside is concentration: a small number of labs, regulators, and major financial institutions become the gatekeepers for tools that could reshape offensive and defensive cyber work at the same time.

There is also a broader technology deal in the market this morning. Axios reports Amazon is buying Globalstar for roughly 11.6 billion dollars, a move aimed at building a stronger rival to SpaceX’s Starlink in satellite internet. Globalstar already has satellite connectivity relationships, including with Apple, and Amazon has been building its own low-earth-orbit network under the Leo name. The deal matters beyond consumer broadband. Satellite internet is becoming part of cloud, telecom, aviation, emergency connectivity, and defense planning. That makes it another example of the same theme running through tech this year: the headline may be software, but the competitive advantage is increasingly physical infrastructure.

Now the market snapshot. U.S. stock futures were modestly higher early Tuesday as investors balanced the Iran blockade against hopes for more talks. Investopedia had Dow futures up about a tenth of a percent, S&P 500 futures up three-tenths, and Nasdaq futures up about half a percent before the open. Oil was lower from Monday’s spike, with West Texas Intermediate around 97 dollars a barrel in that snapshot. Gold was near 4,800 dollars, showing that demand for safety has not gone away. Bitcoin was around 74,400 dollars. Bank earnings are also in the mix, with JPMorgan and Citi beating expectations and Wells Fargo delivering a more mixed report. The short version: markets are trying to look through the blockade, but energy, inflation, and bank commentary can still change the tone quickly.

For Columbus weather, plan on a warm, unsettled spring day. The forecast points to temperatures in the upper 70s to low 80s, with clouds building and scattered showers or thunderstorms possible later today and this evening. Some local forecast guidance flags the chance for gusty winds, hail, quick downpours, and lower visibility on the evening commute, especially around I-70, I-71, and I-270. It is not a guaranteed washout, but it is a keep-the-radar-handy kind of day. Wednesday and Thursday keep rain and storm chances in the forecast before a brief drier window late in the week.

So the through-line this morning is pressure on systems. The blockade is pressure on shipping and diplomacy. Energy prices are pressure on inflation and the Fed. Mortgage rates are pressure on buyers. Columbus is testing pressure on emergency response and public funding. AI is putting pressure on banks, power grids, chip capacity, and satellite networks. None of these stories is settled today, but several have clear next checkpoints: Iran talks and shipping behavior, Fed reaction to wholesale inflation, City Council’s NWSL funding timeline, the May 5th emergency-response ballot campaign, TSMC earnings, and whether storms actually fire in Central Ohio this evening.

That is your Morning Brief for Tuesday, April 14th. Keep an eye on oil, radar, and any new diplomatic signal out of the Gulf.

item.title: Morning Brief — Hormuz Pressure, Housing Friction, and Columbus Sports Math
item.description: The Monday brief follows the U.S. blockade aimed at Iranian ports after failed Islamabad talks, oil and market pressure, a weak March existing-home-sales report, the Central Ohio NWSL stadium debate, AI compute and cybersecurity risks, and a stormy Columbus forecast.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Monday, April 13th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief.

The week opens with the same story we were watching over the weekend, but it has moved from diplomatic risk to operational risk.

The U.S. military says it is beginning a blockade of maritime traffic going into and out of Iranian ports, after the Islamabad talks between the United States and Iran failed to produce a deal.

That distinction matters. President Trump first described a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz itself. U.S. Central Command then narrowed the description, saying the action applies to vessels entering or leaving Iranian ports and coastal areas, while ships traveling through the strait to non-Iranian ports would not be stopped. It is still an escalation. It is still a direct military pressure campaign. And it still sits on top of a fragile two-week ceasefire that was supposed to buy time for diplomacy.

The talks in Pakistan were the continuity story from yesterday. Vice President J.D. Vance left after roughly 21 hours of negotiations, and the core disagreement was unchanged: Iran would not accept the U.S. terms on its nuclear program, while Tehran accused Washington of overreach and said the American demands were not a real basis for peace. Pakistan is still trying to keep a channel open, but there is no settled next round.

So the question this morning is no longer just whether diplomacy can turn a pause into a durable deal. It is whether a blockade can force movement without breaking the pause entirely.

Iran has called the blockade illegal and has warned that pressure on its ports could make no port in the Persian Gulf or Gulf of Oman safe. Israel is backing the U.S. move. Britain and France are trying to keep some distance, with London saying it is not supporting the blockade and Paris and London talking about a separate effort to restore freedom of navigation.

That leaves markets with a messy, high-stakes setup.

Oil jumped back above 100 dollars a barrel in early trading. West Texas Intermediate was quoted around 104 dollars, and Brent was a little above 102. Those numbers matter because the relief rally last week was built on a very simple idea: if the ceasefire held, if the Strait of Hormuz reopened, and if energy prices cooled, the worst of the war premium might come out of gasoline, inflation expectations, and risk assets.

Now that assumption is being tested.

Stock futures were lower before the open, with the Dow, S and P 500, and Nasdaq all under pressure. Early trading was not a clean selloff across every corner of the market. Energy shares had support, and some tech names were trying to stabilize. But the mood is more cautious because the market has to price two things at once: higher oil and the possibility that the blockade becomes the next bargaining chip, not the last one.

The market calendar is busy even without Iran. Goldman Sachs started big-bank earnings today, with JPMorgan Chase, Wells Fargo, Citigroup, Bank of America, and Morgan Stanley coming later this week. Netflix, PepsiCo, Albertsons, and Taiwan Semiconductor are also on the earnings board. Then there is the Producer Price Index tomorrow and the Fed's Beige Book on Wednesday.

That would already be plenty for investors. But every one of those stories now gets filtered through energy, confidence, and inflation.

There is also a trade-risk wrinkle this morning. Over the weekend, Trump threatened a new 50 percent tariff on Chinese imports if U.S. intelligence confirms that China is supplying weapons to Iran. That is not policy yet. It is a threat tied to a contested intelligence question. But it matters because the inflation story does not need many more accelerants right now.

The sequence is the problem. Oil spikes first. Gasoline becomes the household price signal. Then tariff talk raises the chance of another goods-price shock. Then investors and the Federal Reserve have to ask whether consumers will start expecting higher prices to stick.

That is why Friday's sentiment number is still in the background today. The preliminary University of Michigan reading for April fell to 47.6, with one-year inflation expectations at 4.8 percent. Some of that survey happened before the ceasefire, so there was a chance the final number could improve if energy prices cooled. But if the week opens with blockade headlines, crude back over 100 dollars, and new tariff threats, the rebound case gets harder.

For the Fed, the key point is not one bad month of gasoline. Central bankers can look through a temporary energy shock. The harder problem is when households start to believe the shock is durable, and when businesses start pricing around that belief.

The domestic data point to watch today is housing.

The National Association of Realtors said existing-home sales fell 3.6 percent in March to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 3.98 million. That was weaker than economists expected and the slowest pace in nine months. Sales were also down 1 percent from a year earlier.

This is the spring market starting slowly, not just taking a pause. February had given the housing market a little hope because mortgage rates had eased and sales improved. March took some of that optimism back. NAR pointed to lower consumer confidence, softer job growth, limited inventory, and higher mortgage-rate pressure.

The inventory number did improve at the margin. There were 1.36 million homes on the market at the end of March, or about 4.1 months of supply at the current sales pace. That is better than the very tightest period, but still not normal enough to make the market feel easy. The median existing-home price rose 1.4 percent from a year ago to 408,800 dollars, a record high for March.

That combination is the hard part for buyers. Fewer sales do not automatically mean cheaper homes when supply is still thin. People can be priced out at the same time prices keep rising.

For home lending, today's rate picture is a narrow kind of relief. Bankrate's national average for a 30-year fixed mortgage was 6.41 percent this morning, down from 6.50 percent a week earlier. The 15-year fixed was 5.78 percent, down from 5.83 percent. Freddie Mac's weekly survey last Thursday had the 30-year at 6.37 percent and the 15-year at 5.74 percent.

So yes, rates have moved a little lower from late-March stress. But the window is thin. Buyers are still staring at rates above the levels that briefly appeared in February, and lenders are reacting to Treasury yields, oil, inflation, and geopolitical headlines. If the Hormuz story pushes inflation expectations higher again, the rate relief can disappear quickly.

For Central Ohio, this is where the national story becomes local. The Columbus region still has job growth, population growth, and a shortage of comfortable housing options. When rates ease a little, some buyers move fast. When rates bounce, the demand does not vanish. It spreads outward, shifts to smaller homes, or turns into another month of renting. That is why we keep pairing housing with infrastructure and transportation in this brief. The region's growth is not just a skyline story. It is a payment, commute, utility, and land-use story.

Locally, the most immediate developing story is the push to bring a National Women's Soccer League team to Columbus.

Columbus and Haslam Sports Group are competing for an expansion franchise, and the recent City Council discussion is the key local marker. The proposal has shifted from a more direct public-capital-dollar ask toward a possible 2 percent admissions tax on events at ScottsMiracle-Gro Field. The broader plan could include upgrades to the Crew's stadium and a training facility connected to McCoy Park.

Supporters see a chance to make Columbus a serious women's sports market at a moment when women's soccer is growing fast. They point to the Crew infrastructure, local sports appetite, corporate partners, and the city's track record as a soccer town. The bid also has names with real local weight, including Haslam Sports Group, Pete Edwards, and Nationwide.

The friction is also real. Some residents near McCoy Park are worried about what a training facility would mean for existing plans and neighborhood use. Some council members have been skeptical of city capital dollars at a time when the budget is tight. And there is another local layer: the Columbus Eagles have been building women's soccer here since 2013, long before an NWSL bid had this kind of attention. The question is not only whether Columbus can win a franchise. It is how the new professional push would treat the soccer ecosystem that already exists.

That is why this one belongs in the active local file for the week. The council process could still shape the public-funding path, the site path, and the politics of the bid.

The other Central Ohio story still active is data centers and power.

The proposed constitutional amendment that would restrict large new data centers in Ohio still faces a July 1 signature deadline. Backers would need more than 413,000 valid signatures from at least half of Ohio's counties to reach the November ballot. That is a steep climb, but the issue is not going away because the underlying pressure is not going away.

Central Ohio is one of the places where the national AI buildout becomes local electricity demand. The public question is who pays for transmission and grid upgrades when a large share of the need comes from very large users. The Ohio Consumers' Counsel has already been watching proposed utility spending tied to AI data-center load. Residents are also asking about noise, water, land use, tax incentives, and whether local governments have enough leverage.

On the technology side, the demand signal is still strong. TSMC reported first-quarter revenue of about 35.7 billion dollars, up 35 percent from a year earlier, helped by AI-chip demand. Full earnings are due later this week, and analysts are looking for another record profit. That matters because TSMC is the key manufacturer for the most advanced chips used by Nvidia, Apple, and other major customers. When TSMC says AI demand is still pulling hard, it tells you the data-center buildout is not slowing just because local politics are getting louder.

There is a second AI thread this morning: cybersecurity.

Anthropic's new model, referred to in recent reports as Mythos, is not being released broadly because of concerns that its coding and reasoning abilities could be used to find and exploit software vulnerabilities. The company is instead working with a smaller group of partners through a controlled cybersecurity effort. Goldman Sachs said it is working with Anthropic and cybersecurity partners on the risk, and large financial institutions are watching closely.

That is the AI story in miniature. The same capabilities that can help defenders find weaknesses can help attackers move faster. The same enterprise tools that promise productivity can create new operational risks. And the same compute demand that makes chipmakers and cloud providers more valuable is also driving local fights over electricity, water, and public incentives.

So if you are trying to connect the dots this week, the line runs from AI software to chips, from chips to data centers, from data centers to power buildout, and from power buildout to state and local politics.

For Columbus weather, plan for a warm, breezy, and unsettled Monday, but not an all-day washout.

The National Weather Service forecast for the Columbus area calls for a chance of showers and thunderstorms before late afternoon, then only a slight chance after that. The high is near 78, with southwest winds around 17 to 20 miles per hour and gusts that could reach the low 30s. Tonight is mostly cloudy, with another chance of showers and thunderstorms mainly before daybreak Tuesday and a low around 63. Tuesday looks warmer, with partly sunny skies and a high near 83, though another shower or thunderstorm chance returns later.

So the practical version: keep the umbrella close today, expect a gusty feel outside, and watch for quick wet-road problems rather than a steady soaking rain.

The bottom line this Monday is that several of the stories we have been tracking are now converging.

The failed Islamabad talks are feeding the blockade. The blockade is feeding oil. Oil is feeding inflation concern. Inflation concern is keeping mortgage-rate relief fragile. Housing remains slow even when rates improve a little. And Central Ohio is still trying to decide how to pay for growth, whether that growth shows up as homes, rail planning, women's sports facilities, or AI data centers.

That does not mean every headline is the same story. It means the week starts with one shared question: when a region or a country is growing, who absorbs the cost of the capacity it needs?

For today, watch three things.

First, whether the U.S. blockade stays limited to Iranian ports or turns into a broader fight over the Strait of Hormuz. Second, whether oil holds above 100 dollars a barrel and starts to pull gasoline and inflation expectations higher again. Third, whether Columbus City Council gives the NWSL bid a clearer local funding path or keeps pressure on the city to renegotiate.

That's the brief for Monday, April 13th. Be careful in the storms today, and keep an eye on oil before you make any big read from the stock tape.

item.title: Morning Brief — Failed Talks, Fragile Relief, and Columbus Capacity
item.description: The Sunday brief follows the failed U.S.-Iran talks in Islamabad, the fragile market and oil setup for the new week, mortgage-rate relief that may not hold, AI cybersecurity and chip demand, Central Ohio rail and data-center pressure, and a warm Columbus forecast.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Sunday, April 12th, 2026. This is The Morning Brief.

This morning starts with the story we were watching yesterday, and the update is not the one markets, households, or diplomats wanted.

The U.S. and Iran talks in Islamabad ended without a deal.

That does not mean the ceasefire is over this minute. The two-week pause still has time on the clock, and Pakistan is still trying to keep the parties talking. But the first direct, high-level U.S.-Iran meeting in years did not settle the biggest issues: Iran's nuclear program, the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, the scope of the ceasefire, and whether the fighting tied to Lebanon is inside or outside the agreement.

So the right way to hear this is simple. Yesterday, the question was whether diplomacy could turn a temporary pause into something more durable. Today, the answer is: not yet.

Vice President J.D. Vance left Pakistan after a marathon round of talks that stretched about 21 hours. U.S. officials said Iran would not accept core American terms around nuclear limits and the waterway. Iranian officials blamed U.S. overreach and said the demands were excessive. Pakistan said it would keep mediating, but there was no confirmed next round as of early Sunday.

That keeps the Strait of Hormuz at the center of the economic story. It is not just a faraway map point. It is the chokepoint for roughly a fifth of global energy supplies. When it is blocked, constrained, mined, tolled, or politically uncertain, energy markets do not treat that as background noise. They price it into oil, gasoline, shipping, inflation expectations, and eventually household confidence.

That is why this failed round matters before U.S. markets even open for the week.

Stocks finished last week in a strange place. Friday was mixed, but the week was strong. The S and P 500 slipped a tenth of a percent Friday to 6,816.89. The Dow fell about six tenths of a percent to 47,916.57. The Nasdaq rose about four tenths of a percent to 22,902.89. For the week, the S and P 500 gained 3.6 percent, the Dow rose 3 percent, and the Nasdaq jumped 4.7 percent.

That was the relief trade. Investors were willing to buy the idea that the ceasefire had lowered the worst-case risk.

Now comes the test.

The talks did not produce a reopening deal. Oil had fallen sharply during the relief phase, but traders will be watching crude, futures, and Treasury yields tonight and Monday morning for the first read on whether the failed talks bring the war premium back.

This is not a clean risk-on or risk-off story. Tech and AI shares still have momentum. Major indexes just had one of their best weeks in months. Bank earnings and large-company earnings are starting to come into view. But the market setup is now more fragile than it looked on Wednesday because the relief rally was built on the idea that diplomacy was gaining traction.

The domestic economic story is just as fragile.

On Friday, the March inflation report showed the war shock had reached American prices. The consumer price index rose 0.9 percent for the month and 3.3 percent from a year earlier. The headline driver was energy. Energy prices rose 10.9 percent in March, and gasoline jumped 21.2 percent. That gasoline move accounted for nearly three quarters of the monthly increase in the overall index.

Core inflation was not nearly as hot. Excluding food and energy, prices rose 0.2 percent for the month and 2.6 percent from a year earlier. Food was flat. Shelter rose 0.3 percent.

But the problem is that households do not live in the footnote. They live in the headline. They buy gas. They buy groceries. They make the commute. They look at airline fares. They see the conflict and the pump in the same mental picture.

That is why the University of Michigan consumer sentiment reading landed so hard. Preliminary April sentiment fell to 47.6, down from 53.3 in March. If that holds in the final reading, it would be the lowest point in the survey's long history. Year-ahead inflation expectations jumped from 3.8 percent to 4.8 percent, and longer-run expectations ticked up to 3.4 percent.

There is an important caveat. Most of the survey interviews happened before the temporary ceasefire was announced. So if fuel prices ease and people believe shipping is normalizing, the final April number could look less severe.

But that caveat also tells us what matters now. If the ceasefire relief was the reason sentiment might recover, failed talks make that recovery less automatic.

The Federal Reserve does not set mortgage rates directly, and it does not control oil diplomacy. But it does watch inflation expectations. It watches whether households begin to expect price increases to stay high. And it watches whether a one-month energy shock starts to contaminate the broader inflation picture.

That brings us to housing and lending.

Freddie Mac's latest weekly survey gave buyers a little room. The average 30-year fixed mortgage rate fell to 6.37 percent as of April 9th, down from 6.46 percent a week earlier. The 15-year fixed moved down to 5.74 percent. Freddie Mac framed the decline as a positive development that could help the spring buying season.

That is true as far as it goes. A lower rate helps. A nine-basis-point drop changes payments at the margin. It can bring a few buyers back into range, and it can help sellers who are entering what is often one of the strongest weeks of the spring listing calendar.

But this is still a narrow window, not a rescue.

Mortgage rates remain high enough to keep affordability tight, and the reason they dipped is the same reason they may be volatile: war risk, Treasury yields, inflation, and confidence. If markets read the failed Islamabad talks as a reason for oil and inflation pressure to rise again, lenders may not hold that relief for long.

For Central Ohio, that matters because housing pressure is not theoretical. It shows up in where people search, how far they commute, what counties absorb growth, and how much infrastructure has to stretch. The Columbus region has been adding homes, but demand is still larger than the comfortable supply. When rates ease a little, some buyers move quickly. When rates jump back, many do not disappear; they look farther out, look smaller, or wait.

That is the local continuity story: growth is not stopping, but the region is still negotiating the cost of growth.

You can see the same pressure in transportation.

WOSU's recent look at passenger rail in Central Ohio put the 3C and D corridor and the Midwest Connect corridor back on the board. The 3C and D line would connect Cleveland, Columbus, Cincinnati, and Dayton. Midwest Connect would link Chicago, Fort Wayne, Columbus, and Pittsburgh. The planning work is still early, and the timelines are still optimistic. Supporters are talking early 2030s for service, with additional federal planning steps, local match questions, station decisions, and state funding choices still ahead.

So this is not a train announcement.

It is a capacity signal.

Columbus remains one of the largest U.S. cities without passenger rail, and the region keeps adding the kinds of pressures that make mobility harder: more housing demand, more logistics, more job growth, more data centers, and more outward commuting. Rail planning will not fix that next week. But it belongs in the brief because it is one of the few local stories that links housing, work, transportation, and long-term competitiveness at the same time.

The other capacity story is power.

Central Ohio is still the center of Ohio's data-center debate. WOSU and Statehouse News Bureau reporting have tracked the proposed constitutional amendment that would ban construction of large new data centers using more than 25 megawatts. The Ohio Ballot Board let that proposal move as a single issue, which means backers now face the harder task: collecting more than 413,000 valid signatures from at least half of Ohio's counties by July 1st if they want to reach the November ballot.

That is a steep climb. It is also not going away.

The public concern is not only about buildings. It is about electricity, water, noise, tax breaks, transmission lines, local control, and whether ordinary customers end up paying for infrastructure built around AI demand. The Ohio Consumers' Counsel has already warned that a $1.1 billion AEP Ohio and FirstEnergy transmission proposal could put a large share of the cost on customers, with filings tying much of the need to AI data centers.

And AI demand is still sending the opposite signal from the local backlash.

TSMC, the world's largest contract chipmaker, reported first-quarter revenue of about 1.134 trillion Taiwan dollars, or about 35.71 billion U.S. dollars, up 35 percent from a year earlier and ahead of market forecasts. The simple read is that demand for advanced AI chips remains very strong, with Nvidia and other major customers still driving the cycle.

That is the global signal: more AI, more chips, more compute.

The local signal is: who pays for the physical footprint?

The other AI story this week is not just scale. It is control. Anthropic is limiting access to its new Mythos Preview model because of cybersecurity risk, making it available only to a small group of vetted organizations and partners under a defensive-security effort. Axios has reported that OpenAI is also preparing a cybersecurity-focused product for a limited set of partners. The concern is that frontier AI models are becoming more capable at finding and exploiting software vulnerabilities, including in critical infrastructure.

That matters for two reasons.

First, it explains why AI infrastructure demand is not slowing. Companies are still racing for chips, data centers, and model capability.

Second, it explains why the public conversation is getting harder. If the same technology needs more power and creates new cybersecurity risk, then the argument cannot simply be that more compute is automatically good. Communities will ask what they get, what they pay, what safeguards exist, and who is accountable if something breaks.

That is why the Ohio data-center story and the AI cybersecurity story belong together.

Before we close, the Columbus weather.

The National Weather Service forecast for the Columbus area points to a warm Sunday, with mostly sunny to partly sunny conditions and highs around 80 to the lower 80s. Tonight stays much milder, around the low 60s. Monday brings a chance of showers, more clouds, and another warm day near 80, with some breeze.

So Central Ohio gets a spring preview today. It is the better part of the local picture: warm enough to use, mild enough tonight, and not yet the rainy start to the workweek.

Here is the bottom line this morning.

The biggest national story is that U.S.-Iran talks in Islamabad ended without a deal, leaving the two-week ceasefire alive but more fragile and leaving the Strait of Hormuz unresolved.

The biggest market story is that stocks had a strong relief week, but the failed talks now put oil, futures, and Treasury yields back on watch before Monday's open.

The biggest economic story is that March inflation was energy-driven, consumer sentiment hit a record-low preliminary reading, and inflation expectations moved in the wrong direction.

The biggest lending story is that mortgage rates eased to 6.37 percent, but the relief depends on whether bond markets keep treating the energy shock as temporary.

And the biggest Central Ohio story is capacity: housing demand, passenger rail planning, data-center power needs, and AI infrastructure are all different versions of the same question, which is how fast this region can grow without pushing too many costs onto households.

So watch three things today and tomorrow.

Watch for any sign of a new U.S.-Iran negotiating channel, because no confirmed next round means the ceasefire gets harder to trust.

Watch oil and Treasury yields when markets reopen, because that is where the failed-talks story will hit the U.S. economy first.

And watch the local data-center and rail debates, because Central Ohio's growth story is no longer abstract. It is showing up as power lines, water use, housing math, commute patterns, and ballot language.
